ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM covers grilled beef meat broadly; no rump-specific sub-code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of grilled beef rump steak is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one restaurant-style steak portion.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, rump steak, grilled — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for grilled beef rump steak (F06030032). All core macros and micronutrients sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (beef rump, cooked) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat fractions.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Grilled beef rump steak contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI value is assigned. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (41 mg/100 g) and high potassium (326 mg/100 g) yield a favourable Na:K ratio for blood pressure management.
hypertension.guidance: Grilled beef rump steak is suitable for hypertensive individuals when consumed without high-sodium sauces or marinades; its low sodium and high potassium content align with DASH dietary targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar produce no postprandial glycaemic spike; high protein (29.9 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled beef rump steak is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contributes no dietary carbohydrate; portion size should be moderated to limit saturated fat intake, and sugary marinades or glazes must be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.2 g/100 g) and ruminant trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) contribute to LDL-C elevation and pro-inflammatory signalling relevant to atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit grilled beef rump steak to occasional consumption (no more than 1–2 servings per week), trim visible fat before grilling, and balance intake with omega-3-rich fish to offset the saturated and trans fat burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High arachidonic acid (omega-6) content drives TNF-alpha and IL-6 pro-inflammatory pathways; ruminant trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) further amplifies inflammatory signalling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat consumption and substitute with oily fish (salmon, mackerel) to shift the omega-6:omega-3 balance toward anti-inflammatory eicosanoid production.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled beef rump steak is a cut of red meat taken from the hindquarter rump portion of beef cattle, grilled over dry heat until cooked through with a slightly charred exterior. The rump is a lean, firm muscle with moderate intramuscular fat and attached external fat, yielding a robust, savoury flavour and a dense, slightly chewy texture. Per 100 g, it provides 217 kcal, 29.9 g protein, 10.8 g fat (approximately 4.2 g saturated), 0 g carbohydrate, 41 mg sodium, and 326 mg potassium. The high protein content supports muscle maintenance and satiety. The absence of carbohydrates makes it glycaemically inert, suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. Sodium is very low, aligning with DASH guidelines for hypertension management. However, saturated fat and ruminant trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) warrant moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis. Grilled rump steak is a staple of Western cuisine, commonly served as a main course at steakhouses and in home cooking across Australia, the United Kingdom, and Singapore. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal when sourced from certified suppliers.
common_user_queries: How many calories in grilled beef rump steak?
Is rump steak good for diabetics?
Is grilled beef steak high in protein?
Is rump steak low in sodium?
Can I eat rump steak on a keto diet?
How much fat is in grilled rump steak?
Is rump steak suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database entry F06030032 at confidence 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source with an exact match to the food as described.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero: grilled beef rump steak is a pure animal-muscle food containing no dietary carbohydrate; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F06030032. Sugar_g is naturally zero as no sugars are present in plain grilled beef. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as animal muscle tissue contains no dietary fibre. Saturated_fat_g of 4.2 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef rump (cooked, roasted) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; this is documented as an adapted value. Trans_fat_g of 0.3 g/100 g represents naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (vaccenic acid / conjugated linoleic acid) estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked beef rump. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, moisture, and extended micronutrient panel data for this specific HPB entry are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central at the rump-grilled specificity level as of 2026-04-16. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g); glycemic_index set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable per critical rule. This is a Western cuisine food; no Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese multilingual naming requirements apply under G6. FoodEx2 match type corrected to 'closest_parent' (from 'adapted') to conform to allowed values: ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].
